data.title: Nordea Lifts Dividend, Will Continue to Seek Buybacks
data.description:
By Dominic Chopping


Nordea Bank raised its full-year dividend and said it will continue to seek opportunities to distribute excess capital to shareholders in the form of share buybacks.

The Helsinki-based bank on Thursday posted net profit attributable to shareholders of 1.16 billion euros, equivalent to $1.39 billion, for the fourth quarter, up slightly from 1.13 billion euros a year earlier, while net interest income slipped 4.8% to 1.77 billion euros.

A poll of analysts by FactSet had forecast net profit of 1.16 billion euros and net interest income of 1.75 billion euros.

Nordea said the net interest income decline followed central bank policy rate reductions, while costs fell 3% as strategic investments were stable and the bank continues to actively manage costs.

Mortgage lending grew 1%, driven by Sweden and Norway, and corporate lending growth was strong, it said.

The bank proposed a full-year dividend of 0.96 euros, up from 0.94 euros in 2024. The board has also proposed a midyear dividend in 2026, corresponding with around 50% of the net profit for the first half of 2026.

Its common equity Tier-1 ratio was 15.7%, compared with 15.8% a year earlier.

Nordea expects a return on equity above 15% in 2026, after reporting a figure of 15.5% in 2025, and a cost-to-income ratio excluding regulatory fees of around 45%.


Write to Dominic Chopping at dominic.chopping@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

Sanofi targets high single digit sales growth in 2026, plans share buyback
data.description:
LONDON, Jan 29 (Reuters) - French drugmaker Sanofi said on Thursday it expects sales to grow by a high-single-digit percentage in 2026, banking on strong demand for its blockbuster asthma drug Dupixent and some newer medicines.

The company also said it expects business operating income to grow slightly faster than sales this year, and plans to buy back 1 billion euros ($1.20 billion) in shares. In 2025, Sanofi completed a 5 billion euro share buyback program.

Sanofi has been seeking new products to drive revenue growth once Dupixent goes off patent in 2031, and had laid out plans to be more active with mergers and acquisitions.

Its largest deal in 2025 was a $9.5 billion acquisition of Blueprint Medicines that added an approved drug for a rare blood disorder to its portfolio.

Last year, Sanofi also agreed to buy adult vaccine maker Dynavax Technologies for $2.2 billion in December, and closed its $1.5 billion acquisition of British private vaccine developer Vicebio in July.

For the fourth quarter, business operating income was 2.34 billion euros, compared to 2.37 billion euros expected on average by analysts in a company-provided poll.

Quarterly sales of Dupixent, which Sanofi makes with partner Regeneron, were 4.25 billion euros, compared with 4.05 billion euros expected on average by analysts.

($1 = 0.8345 euros)

(Reporting by Bhanvi Satija in London; Editing by Christian Schmollinger)

data.title: Taiwan's assets under management for high-net worth clients soars to T$2.1583 trillion as of year end 2025
Meta Platforms: Reassures Markets With Strong Results and Growth Outlook for 2026
data.description:
Meta shares rise over 9% in after-hours trading on Wednesday, driven by better-than-expected quarterly results and an upbeat outlook for the start of the year. The group posted EPS of $8.88 in Q4 2025, versus $8.23 expected, on revenue of $59.89bn, up 24% y-o-y. For Q1 2026, Meta forecasts revenue of between $53.5bn and $56.5bn, well above the consensus of $51.41bn. Daily active users reached 3.58 billion, in line with expectations.

Despite these performances, Meta is anticipating a sharp increase in spending in 2026, between $162bn and $169bn, including up to $135bn in capital expenditures, mainly for artificial intelligence. These figures are well above market expectations and reflect the group's ambitions in advanced AI, via its Meta Superintelligence Labs and the TBD structure, strengthened by talent from Scale AI. The group is currently testing a new AI model, "Avocado,” the successor to Llama, with a release expected in H1.

At the same time, Reality Labs, the virtual reality (VR) division behind Meta Quest headsets, continues to weigh on Meta's results with a loss of $6.02bn in Q4 on $955m in revenue, figures above analysts' estimates. Over 1,000 job cuts were announced in early January in that division, marking a refocus toward AI and connected devices such as Ray-Ban Meta glasses. While growth in the virtual reality market remains slow, Meta says it is not abandoning the segment. The group also warns of looming legal and regulatory risks, notably in the US and the EU, which could lead to significant financial losses.

data.title: IBM shares rise after solid results and upbeat outlook for 2026
data.description:
IBM shares jumped nearly 7% on Wednesday, lifted by quarterly results that beat expectations and an encouraging guidance for FY 2026. The group posted Q4 EPS of $4.52, above the $4.32 expected, along with revenue of $19.69bn, up 12% y-o-y. Net profit rose to $5.6bn from $2.92bn in the same period of 2024, reflecting a sharp improvement in profitability.

For 2026, IBM now forecasts revenue growth of over 5%, slightly above expectations, although down from the 8% recorded in 2025. The company also expects  free cash flow to increase, which should exceed $15.7bn this year. Chief Executive Arvind Krishna hailed a "solid" 2025, marked by gains across all business lines, notably in generative artificial intelligence, where revenue reached $12.5bn.

In Q4, software revenue rose 14% to $9bn, supported by automation and data solutions and Red Hat products. The infrastructure segment climbed 21% to $5.1bn, driven by the performance of the IBM Z Systems line (+67%). Finally, IBM announced an increase in its quarterly dividend to $1.68 per share, marking 110 consecutive years of payments, an historic record in the technology sector.

data.title: Swedbank's Operating Profit Beats Expectations, Proposes Special Dividend (Update)
data.description:
(update: more details throughout the story)

Swedbank has reported revenues and operating profit for the fourth quarter of 2025 that exceeded analysts' expectations. The board is proposing a special dividend.

Total revenues amounted to SEK 17,340 million (18,634), beating Bloomberg's analyst consensus of SEK 16,830 million.

Net interest income was SEK 10,775 million (12,274), compared to the expected SEK 10,699 million.

Net commission income reached SEK 4,249 million (4,285), with consensus at SEK 4,263 million.

Net trading income amounted to SEK 982 million (923), with consensus at SEK 731 million.

Operating expenses totaled SEK 6,268 million (6,740), compared to the analyst consensus of SEK 6,585 million.

Credit losses amounted to SEK 335 million. Last year, credit losses were positive at SEK 394 million. Expected credit losses were SEK 487 million.

Operating profit was SEK 10,004 million (10,673), compared to the expected SEK 9,177 million.

Net profit was SEK 8,166 million (8,465).

Earnings per share came in at SEK 7.22 (7.53)

The ordinary dividend is proposed at SEK 20.45 (21.70), while the expected figure was SEK 20.53. In addition, a special dividend of SEK 9.35 (0) is proposed. The total dividend thus becomes SEK 29.80 per share (21.70).

Return on equity was 14.7 percent (16.0) for the fourth quarter and 15.2 percent (17.1) for the full year 2025.

The cost/income ratio, C/I-ratio, was 0.36 (0.35) in the fourth quarter and 0.36 (0.34) for the full year.

2025 was a successful year for Swedbank. The goal of a sustainable 15 percent return on equity was achieved. Costs developed as planned. Credit quality is solid, the bank writes in its year-end report.

At the end of the fourth quarter, the acquisitions of Stabelo and Entercard were completed.

During the quarter, mortgage lending increased by SEK 20 billion. Swedish mortgages accounted for SEK 19 billion of this, of which SEK 17 billion came from the acquisition of Stabelo. In the bank's own channels, lending volumes increased by SEK 4.1 billion, including Stabelo.

– We have thus doubled our market share of new mortgages sold through our own channels in 2025 compared to the previous year. But that is not enough. The goal is to grow at least at the same pace as
the market, commented CEO Jens Henriksson in the year-end report.

– Swedbank is strong and well-positioned for sustainable growth and profitability. We continue to create value for our customers and shareholders in both good times and bad, Henriksson concludes.

LVMH Proposes Expedia CEO Ariane Gorin as New Board Member
data.description:
By Adria Calatayud


French luxury group LVMH said it would propose the appointment of Ariane Gorin, chief executive of online travel agency Expedia, to its board.

The company said late Wednesday that the proposal follows Marie-Laure Sauty de Chalon's decision not to renew her mandate as director after 12 years.

data.title: Timo Ihamuotila proposed as chair of Nokia board
Hyundai Quarterly Profit Halves on Tariff Hit
data.description:
By Kwanwoo Jun


Hyundai Motor reported sharply weaker earnings as U.S. tariffs continued to weigh on South Korea's biggest carmaker in the final quarter of 2025.

Hyundai management said during an earnings call that President Trump's tariffs cost the company an estimated 4.1 trillion won, equivalent to $2.87 billion, last year. The automaker forecast a similar tariff hit in 2026.

The impact was reflected in the latest quarterly earnings, with net profit slumping 52% from a year earlier to 1.184 trillion won, the company said Thursday. The result fell well short of the 2.466 trillion won estimated by analysts in a FactSet poll.

Revenue slipped 0.5% to 46.839 trillion won for the quarter, while operating profit dropped 40% to 1.695 trillion won.

Seoul and Washington last year agreed on a trade deal to lower duties on imports of most Korean products, including cars, to 15% from Nov. 1, but the benefits to Hyundai were limited because it still had to clear accumulated inventory.

Yet months later, South Korea is facing renewed uncertainty over U.S. tariffs as President Trump on Monday threatened to raise the duty back to 25%, saying the country's legislature didn't move fast enough to implement the deal. The pact includes a commitment from Seoul to invest $350 billion in the U.S.

Hyundai said net profit fell 22% to 10.365 trillion won last year despite revenue rising 6.3% to 186.254 trillion won. Its operating profit came in at 11.468 trillion won, down 19%.


Write to Kwanwoo Jun at kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

Airbus expects India to have 2,250 commercial jets in service over next 10 years
data.description:
HYDERABAD, Jan 29 (Reuters) - Airbus said on Thursday it expects airlines in India to have 2,250 commercial jets in service over the next 10 years, driven by resilient economic growth, an expanding middle class and a surge of first-time flyers.

Boeing issued a similar outlook for India and South Asia on Wednesday, projecting that airlines in the region will require nearly 3,300 new aircraft by 2044.

(Reporting by Shivansh Tiwary in Hyderabad; Editing by Jacqueline Wong)

data.title: Nokia's Adjusted Operating Profit Exceeds Expectations (Update)
data.description:
(more information and CEO statement)

Telecom company Nokia reported revenue in line with expectations for the fourth quarter. The adjusted operating profit was better than anticipated. The board proposes an unchanged ordinary dividend.

Adjusted revenue rose by 2.5 percent to €6,130 million (€5,983). The outcome can be compared with Factset's analyst consensus, which was €6,108.

Network Infrastructure achieved net sales growth of 7 percent during the fourth quarter, including a 17 percent increase in optical networks.

“Order intake was strong in both optical networks and IP networks, with a book-to-bill ratio that remained above one, driven by demand from AI and cloud customers,” commented CEO Justin Hotard.

Net sales for Mobile Networks increased by 6 percent during the fourth quarter and remained unchanged for the full year. Cloud and Network Services decreased by 4 percent during the quarter.

Adjusted operating profit amounted to €1,058 million (€1,086), expected was €1,020, with an adjusted operating margin of 17.3 percent (18.2).

The proposed ordinary dividend is €0.14 per share (€0.14). The expectation was €0.14.

The company is targeting an adjusted operating profit of €2-2.5 billion in 2026.

“We see strong demand in network infrastructure as we launch new products, expand our presence in AI and cloud services, and invest for long-term growth. In mobile infrastructure, we see a stable market and are focusing on efficiency and improved profitability. Overall, we expect 2026 to show clear progress toward the long-term goals we presented at our Capital Markets Day in November,” Hotard said regarding the outlook.

data.title: ABB Posts Higher FY25 Net Income, Revenue
Three firms vie for Indian gas firm GAIL's tender to buy into US LNG project
data.description:
GOA, India, Jan 29 (Reuters) - Three companies are competing for GAIL India's tender to acquire up to a 26% stake in a liquefied natural gas project in the United States, its Chairman Sandeep Kumar Gupta said on Thursday.

Last year, GAIL issued a tender to buy up to a 26% stake in an LNG project in the United States, combined with a 15-year gas import deal.

India's largest gas distributor invited initial bids from companies as it seeks to buy equity in an existing LNG liquefaction project or a new project that would be commissioned by 2030 at the latest.

The United States is already the second-biggest supplier to India, one of the world's fastest-growing economies, while Qatar is its largest LNG supplier.

State-run GAIL wants 1 million metric tons of LNG per year from a U.S. plant on a free-on-board basis for 15 years, and has said the deal could be extended by five to 10 years.

India is the world's fourth-largest LNG importer and aims to raise the share of gas in its energy mix to 15% by 2030 from 6% currently.

More supplies are likely to come into the market as prices are "normalising," Gupta said on the sidelines of the India Energy Week conference.

"Supply is not a concern, as more volumes are coming in from Argentina, Canada, Alaska and Qatar," he said.

(Reporting by Nidhi Verma; writing by Mayank Bhardwaj; Editing by Sonia Cheema)

By Nidhi Verma

data.title: Nordea operating profit lags market estimates amid muted sentiment
Gold Demand to Remain Strong as Investors Reassess Risk, WGC Says
data.description:
By Giulia Petroni


Global demand for gold is expected to remain strong this year, driven by lower interest rates, uncertainty in bond markets and persistent geopolitical risks, the World Gold Council said after bullion crossed the $5,000 mark for the first time.

"Gold's appeal as an all-weather hedge relative to fixed income should continue to attract material investor demand into 2026, and possibly beyond," the trade group said Thursday in its quarterly report.

Investors across major economies are increasingly concerned about stressed global economic conditions and fiat currencies. That is pushing them to rethink risk, diversification, and wealth preservation. As a result, gold is gaining renewed attention as a hedge and is being more seriously considered and allocated within portfolios.

"Investors around major nations are having questions and concerns around their economic conditions," said Joseph Cavatoni, senior market strategist at the WGC. "I think that's what's helping all the other factors that we know...political tensions, geopolitical tensions, trade relations."

The precious metal has smashed records over the past year, bolstered by central-bank buying and strong inflows into exchange-traded funds as investors shy away from sovereign bonds and currencies in favor of hard assets.

Gold futures in New York climbed above $5,200 a troy ounce on Wednesday, touching an intraday high of $5,306 an ounce after the U.S. dollar fell to a nearly four-year low, making the metal more affordable for holders of other currencies. Last year, gold gained nearly 65%--its strongest annual performance since 1979.

The rally has prompted major banks to lift their price forecasts. Societe Generale and Deutsche Bank said gold could reach $6,000 an ounce this year. Morgan Stanley expects prices to climb to $5,700 an ounce in the second half, while Goldman Sachs forecasts $5,400 in December.

The WGC anticipates another year of continued central-bank purchases, strong ETF inflows, and solid bar and coin demand due to persistent economic and geopolitical uncertainty.

Mine supply and gold recycling are expected to be broadly in line with last year's levels, with high margins encouraging production. Jewelry spending should hold up, but higher prices will limit the amount buyers can purchase.

Global demand for gold accelerated in the fourth quarter of last year, reaching 1,303 tons on hefty ETF inflows and bar and coin buying hitting a 12-year high. Total gold demand in 2025--including over-the-counter transactions--exceeded 5,000 tons for the first time, according to the WGC.

Holdings of gold-backed ETFs surged to an all-time high last year, while central-bank purchases picked up in the fourth quarter. Still, higher valuations appeared to encourage a more cautious pace of buying, underscoring that central banks remain sensitive to price levels even as their long-term strategic interest in gold remains intact.

"I think it is important that central banks are pretty savvy around where their entry point is," said Cavatoni. "If they're running up against the 22% price increase year-to-date, they may pull down their buying programs to see if they can buy a dip, or they can buy when prices stabilize."

Jewelry demand volumes instead declined across all major markets, reflecting the impact of high prices. "When things get expensive, it's not a surprise for us to see tonnage down, particularly in India and China," according to the strategist.

In those markets, however, gold jewelry continues to function as a form of savings. The slowdown is more visible on the fabrication and jeweler side, where high prices have made retailers more cautious about holding large inventories, leading them to scale back upfront purchases.

"The dollar value is still there, so we're not concerned about the jewelry space just yet," Cavatoni said.


Write to Giulia Petroni at giulia.petroni@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-29-26 0114ET
